item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that're used in this article were created for the article, and thus are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, you guys went all in with the chemistry, and all for this object.

I like the idea of a thing that gives useful suggestions to a corporation, but I'm lukewarm on the spooky awareness. except for this idea: This thing is apparently very aware of its environment. What if this thing had been giving chemical structures to its owners because it was in a chemistry research lab, and had been doing it in containment because that is the context in which foundation is interacting with it?

I don't like addendum a. The redactions make in universe sense, they fulfill their purpose, but they are still super frustrating to read.

8.5x11 is fine. That's a standard paper size. It's normal to use units outside of one's organization's adopted standards in context. It's be pointless to write 21.59cmx27.94cm instead. Also technically wrong for bla bla reasons like figure precision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Between the odd tense used in the containment procedures (almost suggesting it is yet to be contained) and the Addendum A which effectively consists of [REDACTED] I really didn't like this one. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, I really do. Unfortunately, the redactions in Addendum A really kill it for me.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In some parts (specially the beginning of the article) the english seems kind of… off, not wrong just… not right the kind of english a foreigner with an advanced but not quite native english level would use. I am sometimes guilty of that mistake myself, mostly when I'm very tired (That is most of the time, being that I usually read scips after 1:00 or 2:00 AM, local time). It gets progresively better towards the ending of the scip, weird stuff.

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted it because I liked it. Then I read the discussion and wished I could vote again beccause it started the phycisists yelling at each other. In my estimation, any SCP that can get people to yell at each other about how science doesn't work is obviously doing SCP right. I imagine Foundation researchers have nothing but these sort of discussions.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Steeltitan is correct. Admins and mods are the only members able to delete posts and threads, and only do so when rules have been broken. It's encouragement to think before you post, or at least make use of that edit button correctly. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is the option, but only admins can actually use it, I think. They usually do it only if something really controversial or bad gets commented, so your comments will probably stay.

item.title: i dont understand
item.content:encoded: I don't quite understand the meaning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't there an option to delete your own comment if you click on the "options" link at the bottom right corner of it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'one a circle about 0.3mm in diameter' is in crass disagreement with the pictures.

I wanted to program a SCP-1336 inspired screensaver, and wanted to stay true to the original. However, the "photos" look much more like 4 or 5 mm. Please use a value like 4.8 instead.

EDIT: Whoops, forgot a factor in my program. The given ".9mm" is absolutely exact. Can someone delete this (my) post?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has sustained very slight burn damage to one corner.

Do you think this should be changed to "the bottom right corner" just for sake of identification/ease?

item.title: inches AND millimeters?
item.content:encoded:
The text says 'SCP-1336 is a single sheet of 8.5" x 11" 110 lb' which is a lot of US customary units.

But later on you say 'one a circle about 0.3mm in diameter' and 'speed is a constant 1mm/s' which sounds more like SI units.

I'm not sure whether that's intentional. And most importantly, I'm not absolutely sure which system of units is the "default" for SCP, but I hope it's SI.

Anyway, here's possible drop-in replacements:

  • 'SCP-1336 is a single sheet of US Standard Letter (8.5" x 11") 110 lb' (If you really have to stick to the paper size but cant' mention it in millimetres)
  • 'SCP-1336 is a single sheet of DIN A4 (210 mm x 297 mm) 80 g/m²' (If you want to go with international paper sizes)
  • 'one a circle about 0.012" in diameter' (If you really need to go with inches)
  • 'one a circle about 1p in diameter' (Appropriate unit below inches — wait. Was that intentional? One point is "one point" thick?)
  • 'speed is a constant 0.04 in/s' (If you really need to go with inches)
  • 'speed is a constant 2.8 p/s' (Appropriate unit below inches)

item.title: Re: Why only aproximately 5000 dots?
item.content:encoded: Without a more accurate measurement, how can they know if the dots don't change color, or if they really aren't multiplying or dieing off, or even escaping the paper?

item.title: Re: Why only aproximately 5000 dots?
item.content:encoded:
They could be more precise, perhaps, but there's absolutely no reason to believe that it would matter. Researchers spending time counting the dots on a piece of paper to get another ~10% accuracy are researchers who could be doing virtually anything else.

I mean, I agree that the Foundation has a tendency to be irrationally rational, so to speak, but that would be ridiculous. 5000 is a good enough estimate, probably.

item.title: Why only aproximately 5000 dots?
item.content:encoded:
Has there been any attempts to catalog all the dots (using some sort of colorimeter or non-destructive forms of spectrography, to distinguish between closely-colored ones), or at least make a catalog of all the unique colors present, and get a histogram of the whole paper for a estimative of the distribution of colors and number of dots?

It seems a bit unusual for the foundation to be satisfied with only a vague description of an artifact/entity when they have the means to be much more precise…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the feeling that we really don't understand this thing at all. I feel like this is what several other SCPs used to look like before we figured out half the stuff in their articles.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded: Well, no, not high school. 8th grade. It's a bit more simplified (and sometimes the oversimplification leads to errors. For example, we don't discuss electron orbitals, we simply state that electrons orbit in specific patterns around the nucleus. I explain to my students that I am oversimplifying, but the reality is probably beyond their grasp until they've had a few more math classes.) Since I wrote this one during our Chem unit, I ended up using an oversimplified language. I'll have to watch that.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
I fixed the chemical jargon. (Genesplicer, aren't you a high school chemistry teacher? You should know better!)

It's not that they have elements that are not on the periodic table. It's that the formulas contain symbols that are not known to represent elements or functional groups.

And don't say that the chemicals are "impossible to synthesize". Just because we don't know how to synthesize a given molecule doesn't mean it's anomalous. The point is that the molecules should be impossible according to currently-accepted theories of chemical bonding.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded: I'd just like to +1 this.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
Um, that's the thing with this SCP.
It displays a bunch of elements and reactions associated with them, evidently using rules that in some ways are like our chemistry and in some aren't.

For all we know, the compounds are indeed implausible and might not be real. Or they work in an universe with subtly different physics, and the thingy was misprogrammed. Who knows.

My point is that this SCP is the type of usage of non-existant elements that doesn't scream physicsfail , as it never asserts such exist. In fact the absurdity of them is what makes the thing interesting.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
implausible.

You do remember where you are, right? alien elements is hardly the weirdest or most implausible thing to be on here.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded: I didn't say that the Foundation had made such compounds. I said that the idea of compounds made with elements currently unknown (as the compounds the SCP displays are) is implausible.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
Point of order: The "islands of stability" in high-number elements that Terralthra dismises so readily do theoretically exist. Particularly, whenever you get to the end of a new periodic table row, you would come to a new noble gas. We haven't gotten there yet, but there is every reason to believe that the new nobles will have the same inert stability as their more common cousins. There are a few other places where particle physics collides with chemistry to make the relevant eggheads think a usefully long-lived element may be formed. But I'm too lazy to try and find the Popular Science article on the whole thing right now.

Not to get overly nitpicky, but chemically inert is not the same thing as atomically stable. Unuoctium (atomic number 118, theoretically a member of group 18, the noble gases) still has a half-life measured in milliseconds. Of note, due to relativistic effects, unuoctium is not actually a noble gas, being solid at room temperature and theoretically reactive with electron-donating elements (e.g. hydrogen) or with ring-formations of reactive gases (e.g. UuoF2, UuoF4).

I don't readily dismiss them, I said they could theoretically exist. The phrase "do theoretically exist" is not really an accurate way of phrasing it. Some theories propose such an island exists, other theories do not. Without any observation, the existing trend of atomic stability doesn't point in a positive direction. As a side point, even the theories that do propose an island of high atomic-mass stability put forth that they are high only by comparison with existing high-atomic mass elements; they would still have half-lives measurable in seconds, days at the most.

Enough real science, now for the sci-fi reasoning. Right now, large atoms are formed by slamming smaller atoms into each other at stupidly high speeds. This is like assembling legos by shooting them at each other out of rifles. Yes, they stick together, but is it any surpirse that the resulting amalgamation is unstable? Now imagine that we get smart enough to combine nuclei at much lower energy levels (i.e. not a head-on collision at speeds measured in fractions of the speed of light). Insert "heisenberg compensator" black-box technology here. This new "cold" atomic synthesis could very well lead to trans-uranics with a stable, chemically-compoundable, shelf-life. Maybe the magic dots from the super-lab know something about molecular synthesis that we don't. Just saying.

This is also technically inaccurate. Elements with high atomic numbers are not unstable due to the circumstances of their creation, but due to interactions of the strong and weak forces on the nucleus. This is borne out experimentally, as when we fuse hydrogen into helium (involving the same incredibly high energy levels), the resulting helium is just as stable as helium found naturally. Further, astrophysics research supports the notion that all elements heavier than hydrogen were originally formed by stellar fusion processes, operating in the cores of older, larger stars which had run out of hydrogen to fuse and instead fused helium into boron, carbon, oxygen, silicon, and so on up to iron. When these stars novaed (or supernovaed), these elements (and even heavy elements like uranium) were scattered throughout the cosmos, to eventually gravitationally accrete into planets made up chiefly of elements such as iron and silicon. Despite their formation in the hearts of a star, at millions of degrees Celsius, with pressures measurable in millions or billions of atmospheres, the atoms formed in these reactions have been stable enough for Terra to be here for 4.5 billion years and counting. The circumstances of formation are not the determining factor in atomic stability and nuclear decay, the mass of the nucleus is.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded: *applauds*

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
Point of order: The "islands of stability" in high-number elements that Terralthra dismises so readily do theoretically exist. Particularly, whenever you get to the end of a new periodic table row, you would come to a new noble gas. We haven't gotten there yet, but there is every reason to believe that the new nobles will have the same inert stability as their more common cousins. There are a few other places where particle physics collides with chemistry to make the relevant eggheads think a usefully long-lived element may be formed. But I'm too lazy to try and find the Popular Science article on the whole thing right now.

Enough real science, now for the sci-fi reasoning. Right now, large atoms are formed by slamming smaller atoms into each other at stupidly high speeds. This is like assembling legos by shooting them at each other out of rifles. Yes, they stick together, but is it any surpirse that the resulting amalgamation is unstable? Now imagine that we get smart enough to combine nuclei at much lower energy levels (i.e. not a head-on collision at speeds measured in fractions of the speed of light). Insert "heisenberg compensator" black-box technology here. This new "cold" atomic synthesis could very well lead to trans-uranics with a stable, chemically-compoundable, shelf-life. Maybe the magic dots from the super-lab know something about molecular synthesis that we don't. Just saying.

We now return you to something you actually care about.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
ie. 453 things. 41 we synthetise because they work, just noone has synthetised them before - 10 have commercial applications, 31 not yet.

Then, we are left with 412 compounds that haven't been synthetised - Out of those, 53 look like nonsense to any chemist, and further 48 look like nonsense to anyone with high school chemistry.

I fail to see how any of this implies foundation's been making compounds with unknown elements in them.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
Right above where you quoted…

Of these, 453 did not appear in any current chemical registry. Since this time, the Foundation has managed to synthesize 41 of the unknown molecules. 10 have proven commercially viable and have been released through various Foundation front companies. The remaining 31 chemicals are still undergoing analysis and testing.

And while it could be that "elements not on the periodic table" refers to alternative names for such, rather than wholly new elements, that doesn't seem like the implication of the passage.

item.title: Re: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
While I appreciate that, you are crying over the wrong grave. This SCP is basically a paper with pictures of compounds that move about and "react", if my tired head tells me right. Some of those symbols don't correspond to real elements. Which is OK because if depicting something, even by non-anomalous means required the thing to exist, there wouldn't be much art around.
And I don't see where it says the foundation actually made them.

Of the 412 compounds that have yet to be synthesized, 53 appear to be physically impossible to synthesize using our current understanding of the nature of chemistry. Another 48 contain elements that do not appear on the periodic table. (Element Za appears in 15 of these molecules.)

In other words, the Foundation hasn't synthetised them yet, and noone suggests they might.

item.title: Unknown elements...
item.content:encoded:
Using "elements that aren't on the periodic table!" always screams "does not understand basic chemistry/physics." There aren't any empty spaces on the periodic table until atomic numbers > 118. The gaps between Hydrogen and Helium don't indicate that there's something in between, they're there so that elements which behave similarly share columns (hydrogen with the alkali metals, helium with the noble gases). Elements above Iron (Fe, 26) are atomically unstable, tending to decay over time inexorably toward Iron, releasing energy as they do. The time it takes for them to decay goes down precipitously the higher the atomic number, with the highest ones there having half-lives measurable only in fractions of a millisecond, and only ever created (as far as we know) with immense apparati of particle accelerators and fusors.

Chemical compounds involving these elements are extraordinarily unlikely (due to the high energy necessary to create them, ionic and covalent bonds would be highly improbable to form, and by the time the energy levels could possibly decrease, the atoms in question would've decayed into something else). Any new element on the periodic table would have to be an even higher atomic number, hence even faster-decaying, hence even less likely to make a compound, ever. The only way that could work is to theorize that for some reason, once you get past the series of elements which decay faster and faster, there's some point at which you hit an "island of stability" in the ridiculously high atomic numbers. All the evidence thus far provides no support to the hypothesis that such an island exists.

If you want to impress upon the reader that this SCP knows about compounds the Foundation can't duplicate, there are more plausible ways of going about it. Complex compounds involving noble gases, for example, are extremely rare: Neon has no known neutral compounds, helium has only a few hypothetical ones, and the rest have only a few hundred possible compounds combined. Making compounds with those would require tremendous technical proficiency, advanced chemistry knowledge, and skillful manipulation, while still being plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1336 is a single sheet of 8 ½ X 11 inch 110 lb cardstock

All of the no. We do not have 8.5" x 11" 110 lb. cardstock at the Foundation.

No opportunity to recover the paper came until several months after the Foundation learned of the existence of SCP-1336, when an explosion severely damaged the lab, killing 15 employees, including the informant. Foundation operatives were able to enter the lab and recover the object at that time.

The Foundation has dozens, if not hundreds, of Mobile Task Forces at its disposal. Each is made up of the best and brightest soldiers the Foundation can find, many of them specially trained in close-quarters, urban combat, and they have essentially limitless funding and sway over local authorities. Why would we wait around and hope the facility blows up when we can just raid the place?

Aside from those points, and a few tone issues, it's actually an interesting idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll see you at the Cubs-Blue Jays series, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, when my Cubbies win, it will certainly be a world-changing event. (Notice, I said "when", not "if".)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm oh-so-very tempted to go with the Cubs. It would certainly herald a CK-class restructuring event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about something rather final like "Apocalypse"?

Assassination.
Impact.
Extinction-level event
Cubs win World Series
Dimensional Crossover
Illuminati displaced

Suggestions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took out the bit about no color matching. Still working on what to put in place of the event redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the addendums a bit, but the core of the article is a bit too long given the ammount of content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly? Figure out a better phrase. "It starts" is utterly nondescript - it might as well refer to the long-awaited television adaptation of Planescape: Torment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could put the phrase in, if you think it would help. What I had originally put in was "It starts." Then I figured that maybe just putting it as a redaction might work better. What do you think? "It starts." or [REDACTED]?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps this is nitpicky, but the redaction of the location investigations and the date/phrase bother me - too little of the redacted information is there to really pique interest. Seems too much like "it knows stuff dammit!" without giving us anything to chew on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this, from the Long Long Ago. I don't remember why, but it tickles at my interest and got a vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this bugged me as well. It didn't contribute much to the article and strained disbelief even further with an object that is already close to A WIZARD DID IT territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A color that can't be matched would be deserving of an SCP number of its own, much like the "missing integer" we have somewhere. Is that necessary to this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Makes sense. Will keep that in mind in the future. However, wouldn't referencing my own work be sorta self-insert? Not that I have a vast store of SCPs to choose from, but I don't really want to do the whole "look what else I wrote!" I just figured that 682 would be a fairly universally recognized object that would show this thing has some sort of awareness and readers wouldn't have to go looking up the reference.

(Again, however, the edit ends up showing that this thing is aware, without the mention of a specific SCP, which, in the long run, is better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, that's all well and good, and you can emphasize it- "SCP 1336 began depicting x other scps which were also contained on site"- but using specific other SCPs that you didn't write is frowned upon because there needs to be purpose in the relationship for the link to be acceptable- it can't be one way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Per advice, reference to 682 has been removed. I was attempting to show that the object is very aware of where it is. I think that is still demonstrated in the edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how to feel about this one, I don't dislike but I don't like it either.

Lose the 682 reference and maybe polish it out a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wise master, you have voiced my complaints in a way I could never have imagined.

In all seriousness though, this is an interesting idea marred by some lackluster execution. And 682 references.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This post is strangely metaphysical, what with the color that isn't a pigment and can't be matched. Gratuitous 682 references are ill-advised, and all 682 references are gratuitous.
